Name: adaptive_latency_optimization
Title: Adaptive Latency-Aware Inference Optimization Using Layer-Skipping Mechanisms
Short Hypothesis: Can we enhance the inference speed and efficiency of deep learning models by introducing a layer-skipping mechanism that adapts to real-time latency requirements during execution without retraining?
Related Work: Existing literature primarily focuses on static optimizations for mixed-precision and hardware-aware inference, but few explore dynamic, real-time adaptations at inference time. Our approach leverages a layer-skipping strategy that can be dynamically adjusted based on the latency constraints, distinguishing it from prior works that rely on fixed architectures or extensive pre-training.
Abstract: In this study, we propose a novel adaptive latency-aware inference optimization framework that utilizes a Jacobian-based layer-skipping mechanism for deep learning models. This approach allows models to dynamically skip layers during inference based on real-time latency requirements, thereby minimizing wall-clock time while maintaining acceptable accuracy. By analyzing the Jacobian of the model's output with respect to its intermediate layers, we can identify which layers contribute most significantly to the output variance. This informs the decision to skip certain layers during inference, effectively reducing computational load and enhancing throughput. Our experiments will evaluate the performance of this framework across multiple architectures and benchmark datasets, assessing metrics such as accuracy, latency, and throughput to determine the optimal balance between speed and model performance. This research aims to establish guidelines for integrating adaptive mechanisms in latency-critical applications, providing a scalable solution for real-time machine learning inference.
Experiments: 1. Implement the adaptive layer-skipping mechanism in a baseline model (e.g., ResNet or Transformer) and evaluate its performance on standard datasets (e.g., CIFAR-10, ImageNet).
2. Measure inference latency and accuracy with and without the layer-skipping mechanism under various real-time constraints.
3. Compare the performance of the proposed method against existing mixed-precision and hardware-aware optimizations, focusing on latency-accuracy trade-offs.
4. Conduct ablation studies to understand the impact of different layers on inference speed and accuracy.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential degradation in accuracy if too many layers are skipped during inference.
Difficulty in real-time decision-making may introduce additional overhead.
The mechanism's effectiveness may vary across different architectures and datasets, requiring extensive validation.
